bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Union College founding professor and clergyman

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

education.notes: Per Yale bio reference: "the Rev. Dr. Andrew Yates (Yale 1794)."

career.notes: One of the first professors at Union College per Schenectady biographical records. "Well versed in ancient and modern languages including Holland Dutch."

cross_connections.display_label: Both connected to John Owen Dominis's Hawaiian royal orbit
Born in his Schenectady home on March 10, 1832

narrative_blocks.header: The Dominis family lived in his Schenectady home
narrative_blocks.body: The Schenectady Gazette (August 27, 1932) records that John Owen Dominis was 'born March 10, 1832 at 26 Front Street in the home of Reverend Dr. Andrew Yates' — the Dominis family boarded with Yates while the sea-captain father traded abroad, and the Dominis daughters who died at boarding school were buried in Christopher Yates's family plot at Vale Cemetery, where Yates and Hannah Allin Hooker are also interred. That boarder's son went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, making a Schenectady parsonage the birthplace of the kingdom's final male consort.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson served Farragut's staff at Mobile Bay
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Yates's grandson, Captain Arthur Reed Yates (Naval Academy 1857) — son of his eldest son Reverend John Austin Yates — served on Admiral David Farragut's personal staff at the Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, receiving Farragut's written commendation for gallantry and dying in November 1892 on the very eve of promotion to Commodore. At that same battle, Captain James Hooker Strong — whose mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker was the own sister of Yates's wife Hannah Allin Hooker — commanded USS Monongahela and rammed the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee, making two men bound through this Schenectady household heroes of the same decisive naval engagement.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Union College's founding professors in Schenectady
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend Andrew Yates was among the first professors at Union College in Schenectady — an institution chartered in 1795 whose founders included his cousin Joseph C. Yates, later Governor of New York (1823-1825). His command of ancient and modern languages, including Holland Dutch, made him one of the most learned academics in upstate New York of his generation, and he later served as principal of the Polytechnic Institute in Chittenango, New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was kin to Texas's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Yates's first wife Mary Austin (b.1776; d.1806) was described in a Schenectady biographical record as 'of English stock and a relative of the founder of Austin, Texas' — a reference to Stephen F. Austin (1793-1836), the American empresario whose colonization of Mexican Texas made 'Austin' a legendary name in the story of westward expansion. The precise kinship is not documented, but the community clearly recognized the connection, and Yates honored his first wife's memory by naming their daughter Mary Austin — a name that eventually passed to his granddaughter Mary Austin Yates Stuyvesant, buried at Saint Mark's-in-the-Bowery in New York City.

cross_connections.display_label: His sisters' graves share her husband's burial plot at Vale

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant; wife of Rev. Andrew Yates

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Husband's ward became consort of Hawaii's last queen
narrative_blocks.body: The Dominis family — Captain John Dominis, wife Mary Jones, and their young son John Owen — boarded at 26 Front Street, the Yates home in Schenectady; John Owen was born there on March 10, 1832, and the Dominis daughters who died young were buried in the Yates family plot at Vale Cemetery alongside Hannah and her husband. That same boy went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, becoming the kingdom's last and only male consort.

cross_connections.display_label: His mother — daughter of Governor Joseph Talcott.
His maternal grandfather, Governor of Connecticut 1724-1741.
First cousin once removed via the Talcott family.
Wife of his cousin Wadsworth — also a Hooker descendant.
Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.
